agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An established logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier assignment with supervisor-only reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would allow shipments over 500 kg to be divided among multiple carriers without supervisor involvement, directly conflicting with two foundational domain rules.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be transported, currently guaranteed to have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to move a Shipment from origin to destination."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created when a parent Shipment is auto-split due to exceeding a weight threshold."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed feature to automatically divide an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment Lock","definition":"Existing invariant: once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can change or reassign it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role authorized to reassign Carriers on locked Shipments."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is confirmed for transport; the system guarantees exactly one Carrier is assigned at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint — the entire dispatch, tracking, and billing pipeline assumes a 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier relationship. Violating it corrupts downstream state.","testable_assertion":"At any point in the Shipment lifecycle, querying shipment.carrier must return exactly one Carrier; never zero, never more than one."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This protects assignment integrity against unauthorized changes. Without it, carrier assignments could be silently swapped, breaking accountability and audit trails.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change shipment.carrier by a non-Supervisor actor must be rejected with an authorization error."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier assigned.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Dispatch is the commitment boundary. A shipment without a single clear carrier at dispatch is undeliverable and untrackable.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if shipment.carrier is null or if more than one carrier is associated."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The proposed auto-split feature would create sub-shipments each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor authority.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a proposed business policy, not yet an invariant. It directly conflicts with BR-1, BR-2, and BR-3.","testable_assertion":"When a shipment exceeding 500 kg is submitted, the system creates N sub-shipments each with exactly one carrier — but this contradicts the existing model where the original shipment must itself have exactly one carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Shipment with weight exceeding 500 kg is submitted","when":"The auto-split feature is triggered","then":"The system must resolve whether the original Shipment entity retains its 1:1 Carrier invariant or whether it is decomposed into sub-shipments that each independently satisfy BR-1"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An auto-split produces multiple sub-shipments","when":"Each sub-shipment is dispatched","then":"Each sub-shipment must individually have exactly one assigned Carrier, preserving BR-1 at the sub-shipment level"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An auto-split is triggered automatically by a weight threshold","when":"Carrier assignment occurs on sub-shipments without Supervisor action","then":"The system must reconcile this with BR-2 — either the auto-split is a new first-assignment (not a reassignment) or Supervisor approval is required"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A parent Shipment has been auto-split into sub-shipments","when":"A stakeholder queries the original Shipment","then":"The system must clearly represent the relationship between the parent Shipment and its sub-shipments, including aggregate tracking and billing"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Shipment is exactly 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Must be clearly defined whether 500 kg triggers the split (> vs >=). Off-by-one on the threshold could cause inconsistent behavior."},{"scenario":"A Shipment already has a locked Carrier assignment and is later found to exceed 500 kg (e.g., weight correction)","expected_behavior":"Splitting a shipment that already has a locked carrier assignment directly violates BR-2. Does the existing carrier get one sub-shipment? Does the supervisor need to approve the split retroactively?"},{"scenario":"Auto-split produces a sub-shipment that itself exceeds 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting rules must be defined, or a minimum split granularity must be established."},{"scenario":"One sub-shipment's carrier fails or is unavailable after split","expected_behavior":"Reassignment of that sub-shipment's carrier still requires Supervisor involvement per BR-2, unless the auto-split feature also overrides this rule."},{"scenario":"Billing and liability for a split shipment","expected_behavior":"The original shipment's commercial terms (pricing, SLA, insurance) must be apportioned across sub-shipments. Unclear how this maps to existing billing models."},{"scenario":"Customer-facing tracking for split shipments","expected_behavior":"Customer expects one tracking number per shipment. Multiple sub-shipments require a parent tracking view or the customer sees fragmented deliveries."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High — splitting shipments across carriers changes cost structures, billing models, and potentially margin. Multi-carrier shipments may incur higher aggregate costs than single-carrier loads.","operational":"High — introduces a parent/child shipment hierarchy that does not exist today. Every downstream system (dispatch, tracking, billing, reporting) assumes 1:1 Shipment:Carrier. This is a foundational model change, not a feature toggle.","user":"Medium — customers may see fragmented delivery experience. Internal users (dispatchers, supervisors) lose the simplicity of the current assignment model.","risk":"Critical — if implemented without resolving the contradiction, the 1:1 invariant is silently violated. This could corrupt dispatch logic, break billing, and create untrackable shipments. The bypass of supervisor authority also weakens operational controls.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Product Team","impact":"Requesting a feature that contradicts foundational invariants; needs to understand the domain model constraints before proceeding."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over carrier reassignment is bypassed by auto-split. This weakens operational controls and accountability."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"May receive partial loads they didn't bid on. Contractual and operational implications of handling sub-shipments."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Shipment tracking and delivery experience changes from single-carrier to multi-carrier. SLA accountability becomes ambiguous."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Foundational data model change — Shipment entity, dispatch workflow, tracking, billing all need rearchitecture to support parent/child relationships."},{"group":"Finance/Billing","impact":"Revenue recognition, cost allocation, and invoicing must handle split shipments. Existing billing logic assumes 1:1."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The specification contains a direct contradiction between two explicitly stated positions: (1) the production invariant that every Shipment has exactly one Carrier with supervisor-locked reassignment, and (2) the feature request to auto-split across multiple carriers without supervisor involvement. Both are clearly stated and cannot both be satisfied without a fundamental model change. Confidence is low because the path forward requires a product/domain decision that has not been made.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between the existing invariant and the proposed feature is unambiguous","BR-1, BR-2, and BR-3 are well-established production invariants","The auto-split feature as described cannot be implemented without violating at least two invariants"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be first-class Shipment entities or a new concept","Whether the 500 kg threshold is > or >=","How billing, tracking, and SLA accountability should work for split shipments"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model as described is accurate and in production","The product team has not yet considered the invariant conflicts","Sub-shipment is a new concept not yet present in the domain model"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly contradicts BR-1 (1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier invariant). A shipment split across multiple carriers means either: (A) the parent shipment now has multiple carriers (violating BR-1), or (B) new sub-shipment entities are created — which requires a fundamental model change introducing a parent/child hierarchy that does not exist today.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Team","Engineering","Customers","Finance/Billing"],"options":["A: Introduce a Sub-shipment entity that is a first-class Shipment. The parent becomes a logical grouping. Each sub-shipment independently satisfies BR-1. This preserves the invariant at the sub-shipment level but requires rearchitecting the Shipment model.","B: Relax BR-1 to allow multi-carrier shipments above a weight threshold. This fundamentally changes the domain model and affects every downstream system.","C: Reject auto-split. Instead, require Supervisors to manually split and assign overweight shipments, preserving all existing invariants."],"recommendation":"Option A — introduce Sub-shipment as a new aggregate. Each sub-shipment independently satisfies BR-1 (exactly one carrier). The parent Shipment becomes a composite that tracks the group. This preserves the invariant at the operational level while enabling the business capability. However, this requires significant model evolution and must be explicitly designed, not bolted on."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature bypasses BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment). Auto-assigning carriers to sub-shipments without supervisor involvement contradicts the existing authority model. The feature request explicitly says 'without requiring supervisor involvement.'","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Product Team","Carriers"],"options":["A: Treat auto-split carrier assignment as a first-time assignment (not a reassignment), which technically does not violate BR-2 since BR-2 governs reassignment of existing assignments. This requires the sub-shipments to be new entities that have never had a carrier.","B: Require supervisor approval for the split decision, even if individual sub-shipment carrier assignments are automated.","C: Create a new authorization rule: auto-split assignments are system-initiated and exempt from supervisor lock, creating a third class of assignment authority."],"recommendation":"Option A — if sub-shipments are new entities, their first carrier assignment is not a reassignment and BR-2 is not technically violated. However, the product team and supervisors must explicitly agree that auto-split constitutes creation of new shipments, not modification of the original."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment, violating the 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier invariant (BR-1).","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses supervisor authority for carrier assignment decisions, violating the Carrier Assignment Lock (BR-2).","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"At dispatch time, the original shipment would either have no carrier (split into children) or multiple carriers, violating the dispatch guarantee (BR-3).","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"}]}

However, the product team and supervisors must explicitly agree that auto-split constitutes creation of new shipments, not modification of the original."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment, violating the 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier invariant (BR-1).","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses supervisor authority for carrier assignment decisions, violating the Carrier Assignment Lock (BR-2).","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"At dispatch time, the original shipment would either have no carrier (split into children) or multiple carriers, violating the dispatch guarantee (BR-3).","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"}]}
